hq: 1267 Page St, Unit C, San Francisco CA 94107

system_description.overview: CentralAxis provides data center infrastructure management software. The platform enables data center operators and colocation providers to monitor and control assets, manage power/cooling/network, and track energy usage in real time. Customers run a Node.js CLI utility agent on-premises that polls SNMP/IPMI devices and reports back to the cloud platform.

products.Automated Data Center Infrastructure Man: Real-time monitoring and end-to-end asset tracking; integration and monitoring capabilities within client environments
products.Unified Power/Cooling/Network Management: Trace energy use through power assets; map network assets together
products.Colocation Metering & Optimization: Granular metering down to tap box and circuit level; real-time power usage and efficiency tracking

infrastructure.cloud_provider: Supabase (carve-out subservice organization — Supabase holds its own SOC 2)
regions: Not explicitly named; Supabase multi-AZ described
infrastructure.physical_presence: No physical offices or servers. Fully cloud-hosted. HQ address listed: 1267 Page St, Unit C, San Francisco CA 94107.
infrastructure.availability_zones: Multi-AZ (Supabase: 'high availability data centers with multiple availability zones'; DR via spinning up multiple servers across all AZs in Supabase)
additional_cloud: Vercel (hosts Next.js app, logging, firewall, observability)
infrastructure.cdn: Vercel (built-in CDN/edge network implied by Vercel hosting)

network_architecture.network_segmentation: System hosted in Supabase in a VPC environment. Network topology includes segmented VPCs and access control lists (ACLs). Supabase provides IDS/IPS in conjunction with security policy network settings.
network_architecture.waf: Vercel (listed in network diagram as providing 'Logging, Firewall, Observability')
network_architecture.firewalls: System firewalls configured on application gateway and production network; Vercel-layer firewall; firewall rules reviewed annually by IT management
network_architecture.ids_ips: IDS and IPS via Supabase (explicitly stated: 'CentralAxis employs IDS and IPS via Supabase')
network_architecture.vpn: Remote access via bastion host with VPN
network_architecture.tls: TLS (version not specified) using certificates from established third-party certificate authority
network_architecture.content_filtering: Content filtering system routed through proxy server (mentioned in endpoint protection section)
network_architecture.topology: End User → Browser UI → [Cloud Services: GitHub (source + CI/CD to Vercel) → Vercel (Next.js S App, logging, firewall, observability) → Supabase (auth, RLS, backups, org data) + AWS (hosts InfluxDB) + InfluxDB (time series metrics store)] ↔ [On-Premises Data Center: Data Center Devices (SNMP, IPMI) → Utility Node (Node.js CLI, SNMP polling)]

frameworks: Next.js (hosted on Vercel per network diagram)
application_architecture.environments: Separate development/staging and production environments confirmed (changes tested in dev/test before production)

databases: Supabase (PostgreSQL — auth, RLS, backups, organization data per network diagram)
InfluxDB (time series metrics store, hosted on AWS)
data_storage.data_warehouse: InfluxDB serves as time-series metrics store; no traditional data warehouse mentioned

authentication_access_control.identity_provider: Supabase (auth + RLS per network diagram; passwords controlled through Supabase policy)
authentication_access_control.mfa: MFA enforced for user accounts with administrative access to production platform; MFA required for source code repository access (GitHub); MFA required for email access
authentication_access_control.access_revocation_sla: Within one business day upon termination
authentication_access_control.password_policy: Controlled through Supabase password policy; periodic forced changes, password expiry and complexity requirements; accounts disabled after limited unsuccessful logon attempts

encryption.at_rest: Cloud provider key management service (CC6.1.5: 'uses its cloud provider key management service to encrypt data at rest')
encryption.key_management: Cloud provider KMS (Supabase/AWS KMS — not explicitly named but cloud provider KMS referenced)

ci_cd_devops.source_control: GitHub (explicitly shown in network diagram; MFA required for access)
ci_cd_devops.ci_cd_tool: Vercel (network diagram: 'CI/CD to Vercel' from GitHub)
ci_cd_devops.branch_protection: Code repository branch rules configured to require additional approval for every merge request to production; developers cannot push directly to production without approval
ci_cd_devops.iac: Configuration management tool used for baseline configurations and rollback capability (tool not named; IaC approach described)
ci_cd_devops.patch_management: CTO sets up automatic patch management; monthly patch management reviews; workstations configured for automatic updates and scanned daily for patch compliance

monitoring_logging.log_aggregation: Vercel (logging per network diagram); logging enabled at application and infrastructure level
monitoring_logging.alerting: Automated alerts configured to notify IT management of high and critical risk events; monitoring tool generates alerts at predefined thresholds; tracked in change-management/ticketing application

third_party_services.purpose: Auth, row-level security (RLS), backups, organization data storage — primary database/backend as a service. Holds its own SOC 2.
Hosts Next.js web application; provides CI/CD pipeline from GitHub; logging, firewall, observability
Source code management; CI/CD pipeline trigger
Time-series metrics store for data center device telemetry

security_tools.antivirus: Endpoint security solution installed on all desktops and laptops; anti-virus definitions updated daily; continuous scanning; Mac workstations and laptops covered; production servers covered with weekly definition updates
security_tools.vulnerability_scanning: Monthly automated vulnerability scans on infrastructure and applications; internal IT Infrastructure team performs scans; pre-release self-assessment scan also performed
security_tools.penetration_testing: Annual, external independent third-party; high/critical findings remediated and added to risk register

untestable: CC2.2.3 — No significant personnel/role changes occurred during engagement period (control could not be tested)
CC3.2.1 — Cybersecurity insurance: no cyber incidents reported during engagement period (could not test operating effectiveness)
CC6.5.2 — Customer data deletion upon termination: no customer terminations during engagement period (could not test)
CC7.3.1 — Incident response: no security incidents reported during engagement period (could not test operating effectiveness)
excluded_criteria: CC6.4 — Physical access to facilities: Supabase is responsible for physical security of data centers; Central Axis has no physical servers
A1.2 — Environmental protections/backup recovery infrastructure: Central Axis has no office/physical servers; managed by building management/Supabase
Processing Integrity trust services criteria — deemed not relevant to the system
Privacy trust services criteria — deemed not relevant to the system
notable_controls: Supabase used as sole named subservice org (carve-out method); Supabase's own SOC 2 reviewed annually
On-premises Node.js CLI agent with SNMP polling is explicitly in scope — unusual hybrid architecture for a SaaS company
InfluxDB on AWS for time-series telemetry — separate from primary Supabase database layer
Vercel provides WAF and observability layer — not a dedicated security tool but multi-purpose
No SIEM named — monitoring appears to rely on Vercel observability + alerting without a dedicated SIEM
Annual external penetration testing confirmed and tested (CC4.1.1)
Daily backups confirmed with multi-AZ configuration tested (CC7.4.2)

infrastructure.rationale: Multi-AZ via Supabase with failover capability. Multiple cloud providers (Supabase, Vercel, AWS) for distinct layers. No explicit multi-region. No CDN beyond Vercel edge. IaC described but tool unnamed. Hybrid architecture (cloud + on-premises agent) adds complexity. Deducted for no named IaC tool, no multi-region DR, and heavy reliance on single subservice org (Supabase).

application_architecture.rationale: Interesting hybrid: cloud Next.js SaaS + on-premises Node.js CLI agent. Next.js on Vercel is modern. InfluxDB for time-series is appropriate. Branch protection and code review confirmed. No languages beyond JS/Node.js disclosed, no testing framework mentioned, no microservices architecture. Architecture is fit-for-purpose for a DCIM tool but not particularly sophisticated.

data_layer.rationale: Good separation: Supabase (PostgreSQL) for organizational/auth data + InfluxDB for time-series telemetry — appropriate dual-database architecture for DCIM. Daily backups confirmed. Multi-AZ. Row-level security (RLS) via Supabase is a strong per-tenant isolation mechanism. No caching layer, no data warehouse, no file storage mentioned. Data retention and classification policies exist.

security_posture.rationale: Strong positives: annual external pen test, monthly vuln scans, IDS/IPS via Supabase, WAF via Vercel, MFA enforced for admin/prod access, endpoint protection on all devices, cybersecurity insurance, server hardening. Weaknesses: no named SIEM, Vercel serves triple-duty (hosting + firewall + observability) which is not a dedicated security stack, no explicit DLP.

devops_maturity.rationale: GitHub + Vercel CI/CD pipeline is modern and well-described. Branch protection with required approvals confirmed and tested. Separate environments confirmed. Emergency change process documented. Automatic patch management with monthly reviews. IaC described (baseline configs as code) but tool not named. No container orchestration mentioned.

bcdr_readiness.rationale: BCDR policy exists, reviewed annually, tested annually (multiple loss scenarios). Daily backups with multi-AZ. DR via spinning multiple Supabase AZ servers. No RTO/RPO disclosed — significant gap for due diligence. No multi-region failover. DR dependent entirely on Supabase availability.

vendor_diversity.rationale: Five named vendors: Supabase (critical), Vercel (critical), GitHub (critical), AWS (high), InfluxDB (high). Healthy diversity across the stack. However, Supabase is load-bearing for auth, data, and DR — single point of failure if Supabase has an outage. GitHub + Vercel create a tightly coupled CI/CD dependency. AWS dependency for InfluxDB adds resilience. Vendor management policy and annual SOC review confirmed.

overall.rationale: CentralAxis is a credible, modern early-stage DCIM SaaS company with appropriate technology choices for its size. The hybrid cloud + on-premises agent architecture is fit-for-purpose and correctly scoped. Strong security basics (pen test, vuln scans, MFA, IDS/IPS) and a clean unqualified opinion with zero exceptions. Key gaps are: no RTO/RPO disclosed, no named SIEM, heavy Supabase dependency for core infrastructure, IaC tool unnamed, no multi-region DR, and a very short 3-month audit period suggesting this is a first SOC 2.

red_flags: RTO and RPO not disclosed — critical gap for any SLA-sensitive enterprise customer
3-month audit period (May–July 2025) is extremely short — this appears to be a first-time SOC 2; limited operating history for controls
Supabase is the sole named subservice org using the carve-out method — Supabase's own controls are excluded from this audit scope, creating reliance on Supabase's separate SOC 2
No SIEM named — logging and alerting appear to rely on Vercel observability + manual review without a dedicated security information and event management system
Four controls could not be tested due to no triggering events during the 3-month period (no incidents, no terminations, no cyber events, no personnel changes)
yellow_flags: IaC tool not named — 'configuration management tool' and 'baseline configurations as code' described but no tool (Terraform, Pulumi, etc.) identified
Vercel serving as WAF + CDN + observability + CI/CD target is a multifunctional dependency — not a dedicated security or observability platform
No caching layer mentioned — potential performance/scalability concern at scale
No SSO for customer-facing access mentioned
Processing Integrity and Privacy trust criteria explicitly excluded — limits assurance scope for customers handling sensitive data
On-premises Node.js agent deployed at customer data centers — represents a security/support surface outside CentralAxis's direct control
AWS used only for InfluxDB — creates a second cloud dependency with potential for fragmentation
green_flags: Unqualified opinion with zero exceptions across all 77 controls tested
Annual external penetration testing by independent third party — tested and confirmed
Monthly automated vulnerability scanning — tested and confirmed
Daily backups with multi-AZ confirmed via onscreen evidence (CC7.4.2 tested via onscreen config inspection)
Supabase IDS/IPS explicitly deployed and confirmed
Row-level security (RLS) via Supabase provides strong per-tenant data isolation
Dual-database architecture (PostgreSQL + InfluxDB) is architecturally appropriate for DCIM telemetry workloads
MFA enforced on GitHub (source code), production platform admin access, and email
Branch protection with mandatory approvals confirmed via sample testing
Quarterly user access reviews confirmed and tested
Access revocation within 1 business day confirmed via terminated employee sample testing
Vendor management program includes annual SOC review of critical vendors
key_observations: CentralAxis is a DCIM SaaS with an unusual hybrid architecture: a cloud control plane (Supabase + Vercel + AWS/InfluxDB) paired with a Node.js CLI utility agent deployed on-premises at customer data centers that polls SNMP/IPMI devices. This agent is explicitly in scope for the SOC 2.

The network diagram explicitly names all five technology vendors (GitHub, Vercel, Supabase, AWS, InfluxDB) — unusually transparent for a SOC 2 system description, giving high confidence in the tech stack.

Supabase is effectively CentralAxis's cloud infrastructure provider, not just a database — it provides VPC, IDS/IPS, multi-AZ, backups, auth, and DR. CentralAxis's own infrastructure footprint is minimal.

The audit was performed by Accorp Partners, a boutique compliance firm that appears to standardize report language heavily across clients. The boilerplate nature of most control descriptions limits unique insights but the system description and network diagram are company-specific.

First SOC 2 with a 3-month audit window: companies pursuing SOC 2 Type 2 for the first time often use a short initial period. Prospective customers should request a renewal audit with a 12-month period before relying on this for enterprise procurement.

InfluxDB for time-series telemetry is an architecturally sound choice for DCIM (power/cooling metrics), but running it on AWS rather than within Supabase creates a data layer split that could complicate incident response and backup coordination.

No container orchestration (ECS/Kubernetes) mentioned — consistent with a small team using Vercel serverless for the web app and Supabase managed services, avoiding operational overhead of container management.
